explanation: A D flip-flop is a JK flip-flop with J = D and K = D', so you only need to invert D on its way to the K input: one NOT gate. Recall the JK table: J=1,K=0 sets, J=0,K=1 resets, which is exactly D=1 and D=0 once K is tied to D'. 'No additional gate' is the trap, since wiring D straight to both J and K gives J=K, which toggles or holds instead of loading.

1110 0000 = 224 and 1100 0000 = 192, so 224 + 192 = 416 = 1 1010 0000. The 8-bit result is 1010 0000 with a 1 carried out of the MSB, which is the overflow bit the question is asking for. Note the carry-out signals unsigned overflow only; read as two's complement it is -32 + -64 = -96, which fits in 8 bits, so there is no signed overflow.

Clustering is what happens when keys pile up on the same hash address rather than spreading evenly, which is what option A describes. Be aware the item is loosely worded: two keys landing on one address is strictly a collision, and B (probing for another slot when the address is taken) is the open-addressing mechanism that actually produces primary clustering. A is the best of a poor option set and is what the key marks.

ipconfig on the server already confirms the server's own IP stack and addressing are fine, so the fault is somewhere on the path between client and server. tracert from the host to the server walks the route hop by hop and shows exactly where it dies, which rechecking the server (C) cannot do. A and B are weaker because a bare ping only gives a pass/fail with no location, and pinging the gateway tests just the first hop.

A counter advances by toggling bits, and the T (toggle) flip-flop toggles its output on every clock when T = 1, so chaining T flip-flops directly yields a ripple counter. A JK with J = K = 1 behaves identically and is why B looks tempting, but T is the element defined for the job and is the keyed answer.

The concentric (on a CD, spiral) rings of data on the disc surface are tracks. Sectors are the angular subdivisions cut across a track, and clusters are groups of sectors the filesystem allocates as one unit, so both are units within a track rather than the circles themselves.

Quick sort is the keyed answer: it averages O(n log n) with the smallest constant factors of the general-purpose comparison sorts and sorts in place, so in practice it is the fastest of the four. Know the caveat, because it is the classic trap in reverse here: quick sort degrades to O(n^2) on already-sorted input with a poor pivot, while heap sort is O(n log n) in the worst case, so 'best' is arguable and depends on whether you mean average or worst case.

The loop prints n, then checks the break. n=5 prints 5, n=4 prints 4, n=3 prints 3 and then hits the break, so output is 543. The trap is the ordering: because cout runs before the if, the 3 is printed before the loop exits, so answer B (54) is wrong.

ARP takes a known IP address and resolves it to the MAC address of the host on the local link, so frames can actually be addressed at layer 2. The trap is option A, which is the reverse mapping (MAC to IP), the job of RARP; DNS resolves names to IPs, not addresses to MACs.
An IEEE 802 MAC address (EUI-48) is 6 bytes = 48 bits, written as six hex pairs such as 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E. 32 bits is the trap, because that is the size of an IPv4 address.

MAC stands for Media Access Control, which is the lower sublayer of OSI layer 2, the Data Link layer. The Physical layer is the trap: it deals with bits, voltages and cabling and has no notion of addressing at all.

ATM uses a fixed-size cell of 53 bytes: a 5-byte header plus a 48-byte payload. The fixed length is the whole point of ATM, since it makes switching and delay predictable, so 'variable length' is the trap for anyone thinking of IP packets.

The BST invariant is that for every node, all keys in its left subtree are smaller and all keys in its right subtree are larger, which is option A and is what makes an in-order traversal come out sorted. Option B inverts the ordering, C describes a max-heap, and D describes a balanced (not a search) tree; a BST need not be balanced.

True. Memory is organised as cells, each with a unique numeric address, and it is precisely this addressability that lets the CPU reach any cell directly in constant time, which is what 'random access' means.
RAM is volatile: it holds data only while powered, and its contents are lost at power-off. ROM and EPROM are the non-volatile counterparts, which is why they, and option B, are wrong here.
The keyed answer is Oracle, which was the leading commercial DBMS by market share at the time this exam was written. Linux is the easy elimination since it is an operating system, not a DBMS at all.
Expand by place value: 1100 in binary = 1*8 + 1*4 + 0*2 + 0*1 = 12. The distractors 8 and 16 are the traps for misreading which bits are set or off-by-one on the exponent.
A LAN is a network, that is, the infrastructure connecting computers, not a computer itself. A file server, a workstation and a PC are all machines that run programs, so the LAN is the odd one out.

Reading the K-map, every cell in the x3 = 0 row is 1 and every cell in the x3 = 1 row is 0. The output therefore depends on x3 alone, and the other variables are covered in the grouping and drop out, giving F = x3'.
